title: 1D DP
description: A new problem beyond Fibonacci: House Robber, teaching the real skill of DP — defining what dp[i] actually means, then finding its recurrence.
objectives: Solve the House Robber problem using 1D dynamic programming
Practice defining a DP state (what dp[i] represents)
Derive a recurrence relation from the state definition
Recognize the 1D DP pattern: dp[i] depends only on a few earlier dp values

sections.text: Tabulation's table[i] = table[i-1] + table[i-2] was already 1D DP — a single array indexed by one variable. The real skill isn't the loop structure; it's figuring out what dp[i] should MEAN for a brand new problem. Let's practice that.
dp[i] represents the maximum money that can be robbed considering only houses 0 through i.

This is the single most important step in any DP problem: clearly stating what dp[i] means BEFORE trying to write its formula. Get this definition wrong, and no formula built on top of it will work correctly.

This recurrence directly reflects the state definition: dp[i] is the BEST of the two choices available at house i, each one built from an already-known smaller answer — exactly Dynamic Programming's optimal substructure requirement.

sections.title: Tracing it
points: dp[0] = 2 (only one house, must rob it)
dp[1] = max(2, 7) = 7 (rob whichever of the first two is bigger)
dp[2] = max(dp[1]=7, houses[2]+dp[0]=9+2=11) = 11 → skip house 1, rob houses 0 and 2
dp[3] = max(dp[2]=11, houses[3]+dp[1]=3+7=10) = 11 → house 3 isn't worth robbing

sections.title: Choosing which parking spots to use, never two in a row
sections.text: Picking which spots in a row of paid parking spaces to use, where you can never use two adjacent spots (some safety rule), naturally means asking at each new spot: is it better to skip this one (keep whatever I already had), or take it (plus whatever I'd built up two spots back)? That's exactly House Robber's recurrence, one decision at a time down the row.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Jumping straight to writing a formula before clearly stating what dp[i] means — this is the step that actually makes the recurrence obvious.
Forgetting the two base cases (dp[0] and dp[1]) — the recurrence for i >= 2 depends on both already being correctly filled in.
Writing dp[i] = houses[i] + dp[i-2] unconditionally — forgetting the 'skip' option (dp[i-1]) means missing cases where robbing house i isn't actually optimal.
Confusing this with a problem allowing ANY subset of houses — the whole difficulty here comes specifically from the adjacency restriction.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: In House Robber, what does dp[i] represent?
questions.answer: The maximum money that can be robbed considering only houses 0 through i.

questions.question: What is the recurrence relation for House Robber, and why?
questions.answer: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], houses[i] + dp[i-2]) — at each house, either skip it (keep dp[i-1]) or rob it (add its value to dp[i-2], since the adjacent house i-1 can't also be robbed).

questions.question: Why is clearly defining the DP state before writing the recurrence considered the most important step?
questions.answer: Because the recurrence formula is derived directly from what the state represents — an unclear or incorrect state definition makes it very difficult to write a correct formula on top of it.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: House Robber demonstrates the real skill of 1D DP: defining a clear state, dp[i] = the maximum money robbable considering only houses 0 through i, before writing any formula. The recurrence follows directly from that definition — at each house, either skip it (dp[i-1]) or rob it (houses[i] + dp[i-2], since the adjacent house can't also be robbed) — and dp[i] takes whichever choice is better. This is the same 1D array pattern as Fibonacci's tabulation, just with a problem-specific state and recurrence instead of a fixed mathematical sequence.

sections.title: Trick to Remember 1D DP
sections.text: 1D DP recipe: 1) Define what dp[i] MEANS. 2) Find the recurrence from that meaning. 3) Fill base cases. 4) Loop upward. House Robber: skip it, or rob it + dp[i-2] — take the best.

sections.title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.
hints: Follow this lesson's recipe: define dp[i] = the length of the longest increasing subsequence that ENDS exactly at index i (not just 'somewhere within 0..i').
Recurrence: dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) over every j < i where nums[j] < nums[i] — extend any earlier increasing subsequence that nums[i] can legally follow.
Base case: every dp[i] starts at 1 (the element alone is a valid subsequence of length 1). The final answer is the MAX across the whole dp array, not dp[n-1].

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: The dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j] for valid j < i) recurrence directly, an O(n^2) nested loop.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Patience sorting: maintain a 'tails' array where tails[k] is the smallest possible tail of an increasing subsequence of length k+1, using binary search to place each element.
